---
title: "Introduction to Python and Text-as-Data"
author: "Samirah Jaigirdar"
date: October 26, 2025
---

*The code in this session was adapted from Will Horn and Yaoyao Dai's classes at ICPSR 2025.*

---

## Why Python for Text Data Analysis?

Python is a general-purpose, object-oriented programming language that emphasizes code readability. It is well suited for deploying machine learning at a large scale. Relevant libraries for data science include:

- **Numpy** -- Large Dimensional Arrays
- **Pandas** -- Data Manipulation & Analysis
- **Matplotlib** -- Data Visualizations

## What is Colab?

[Google Colab](https://colab.research.google.com/) is a browser-based notebook with a free GPU/CPU. It allows you to run Python code in a Jupyter notebook environment without any setup on your device. You can combine executable code with text, images, and LaTeX in a single document, and share the Colab doc with anyone for collaborative editing. Colab also has Google AI built in (if you opt in) that can help fix your code.

When you open Colab, first:

- Mount Google Drive (every time)
- Upload CSV/files on the sidebar
- Load packages and libraries (every time)

```python
from google.colab import drive
drive.mount('/content/drive')
```

---

## Syntax Differences: Python and R

### Blocks by Indentation

In Python, **colon + indentation** defines blocks. No braces. Conditions evaluate to `True` or `False`.

```python
x = 7
if x > 5:            # colon starts a block
    print("big")     # 4 spaces indent
else:
    print("small")
```

### 0-Based Indexing and Slicing

`stop` is exclusive.

```python
nums = [10, 20, 30, 40, 50]
first        = nums[0]      # 10
last         = nums[-1]     # 50
sub          = nums[1:4]    # [20, 30, 40]  (stops before index 4)
every2       = nums[::2]    # [10, 30, 50]
reversed_nums = nums[::-1]  # [50, 40, 30, 20, 10]
```

### Assignment (=) vs. Equality (==)

```python
a = 3      # assign
b = 3
a == b     # True (equality check)
```

### Common Operations vs. R

**Filter:**

```r
# R
df %>% filter(year > 2010, country == "India")
```
```python
# Python
df.query("year > 2010 and country == 'India'")
```

**Select columns:**

```r
# R
df %>% select(country, year, gdp)
```
```python
# Python
df[["country", "year", "gdp"]]
```

**Mutate:**

```r
# R
df %>% mutate(gdp_pc = gdp / pop,
              decade = floor(year / 10) * 10)
```
```python
# Python (pandas)
df.assign(gdp_pc = df["gdp"] / df["pop"],
          decade = (df["year"] // 10) * 10)
```

**Left join:**

```r
# R
left_join(df_left, df_right, by = "id")
```
```python
# Python
df_left.merge(df_right, on="id", how="left")
```

---

## If/Elif/Else

In Python, `colon + indentation` defines blocks. We use `if` for the first test, `elif` for additional mutually exclusive branches, and `else` as the fallback. Strings are **case-sensitive**, so we often normalize with `.strip().lower()`.

### If/Elif/Else Pattern

```python
# if <condition>:
#     <do something>
# elif <another condition>:
#     <do something else>
# else:
#     <fallback>
```

```python
x = 90

if x > 10:
    msg = "x is big"
elif x == 10:
    msg = "x is exactly 10"
else:
    msg = "x is small"

print(msg)
```
```
x is big
```

**Common gotchas and rules:**

- End each `if`, `elif`, `else` line with a colon `:`
- Indentation defines the code block (use 4 spaces; be consistent)
- Conditions are booleans (`True` or `False`)
- Strings are case-sensitive (`"Canada"` ≠ `"canada"`)

### Example: Weather

```python
today_weather = input("Weather today (rainy/sunny/cloudy/other)? ").strip().lower()

if today_weather == "rainy":
    print("Grab an umbrella!")
elif today_weather == "sunny":
    print("Grab some shades!")
elif today_weather == "cloudy":
    temp = input("Cloudy—cold or hot? ").strip().lower()
    if temp == "cold":
        print("Wear a sweater!")
    else:
        print("Wear a long sleeve!")
else:
    print("Wear whatever you like!")
```

---

## Loops

**While** loops repeat until a condition changes. **For** loops repeat a known number of times (e.g., over a range or a list). `range(start, stop, step)` excludes `stop` — off-by-one errors are common.

### While Loop

```python
# while <condition>:
#     <do stuff>
#     <update something>  # prevents an infinite loop

countdown = 3
while countdown > 0:
    print("T-minus", countdown)
    countdown -= 1  # update!
print("Liftoff")
```
```
T-minus 3
T-minus 2
T-minus 1
Liftoff
```

If you run a loop and it gets stuck in an infinite loop, hit the **stop** icon on the cell or **Restart runtime**.

### For Loop

Use when you know how many items/iterations. With numbers: `range(start, stop, step)` (`stop` is exclusive).

```python
for i in range(1, 6):  # 1,2,3,4,5
    print("i =", i)
```
```
i = 1
i = 2
i = 3
i = 4
i = 5
```

You can also loop over characters in a string:

```python
for char in "Georgetown":
    print(char + "!")
```
```
G!
e!
o!
r!
g!
e!
t!
o!
w!
n!
```

---

## Lists and Dictionaries

### Lists

Lists use square brackets `[]` and have order. They can hold any mix of items and allow duplicates.

- Create/see length: use `len(...)` to count items.
- Access: zero-based indexing (`items[0]`) and slicing (`items[a:b:c]`; stop is exclusive).

Common actions:

- Add one item: `.append(...)`
- Add many items: `.extend([...])`
- Insert at a position: `.insert(index, value)`
- Remove & return last (or by index): `.pop()` / `.pop(i)`
- Membership test: `x in items`

When to use: ordered data, sequences (e.g., tokens in a sentence, a list of URLs to scrape).

### Dictionaries

Dictionaries use curly braces `{}` and store key → value pairs. Keys are unique; order isn't the point (though Python preserves insertion order).

Common actions:

- Create: `{key: value, ...}`
- Add / update: `dict[key] = value`
- Merge: `.update({...})`

Lookup:

- Direct: `dict[key]` (errors if missing — good when a missing key means your data is broken and you want to fail fast)
- Safe: `dict.get(key, default)` (returns the default if key is missing; no error)

When to use: fast lookups, mappings (e.g., country → capital, word → count, url → last_seen).

**Important:** dictionaries are case-sensitive (`"canada"` ≠ `"Canada"`).

### Slicing and Filtering

```python
fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry", "date", "elderberry",
          "fig", "grape", "grapefruit", "strawberry", "peach",
          "raspberry", "watermelon", "lychee", "pear", "acai"]

# Filtering with a for-loop
short_fruit = []
long_fruit  = []

for fruit in fruits:
    if len(fruit) < 6:
        short_fruit.append(fruit)
    else:
        long_fruit.append(fruit)

print("Short-name fruits:", short_fruit)
print("Long-name fruits:", long_fruit)
```
```
Short-name fruits: ['apple', 'date', 'fig', 'grape', 'peach', 'pear', 'acai']
Long-name fruits: ['banana', 'cherry', 'elderberry', 'grapefruit', 'strawberry', 'raspberry', 'watermelon', 'lychee']
```

```python
# Slicing examples
print("First 3:", fruits[:3])
print("Last 3:", fruits[-3:])
print("Every other:", fruits[::2])
print("Middle block (3..6):", fruits[3:7])
print("Reversed:", fruits[::-1])
```
```
First 3: ['apple', 'banana', 'cherry']
Last 3: ['lychee', 'pear', 'acai']
Every other: ['apple', 'cherry', 'elderberry', 'grape', 'strawberry', 'raspberry', 'lychee', 'acai']
Middle block (3..6): ['date', 'elderberry', 'fig', 'grape']
Reversed: ['acai', 'pear', 'lychee', 'watermelon', 'raspberry', 'peach', 'strawberry', 'grapefruit', 'grape', 'fig', 'elderberry', 'date', 'cherry', 'banana', 'apple']
```

### Dictionary of Lists

A common structure is a **dict of lists**: a category key pointing to a list of members. This is great for lookups, counts per group, and nested iteration.

```python
democrats  = ["Kamala Harris", "Zohran Mamdani", "Bernie Sanders", "Pete Buttigieg"]
republicans = ["Greg Abbott", "Ted Cruz", "Marco Rubio", "George Bush"]

political_dictionary = {
    "Democrat":   democrats,
    "Republican": republicans,
}

print(political_dictionary)
```
```
{'Democrat': ['Kamala Harris', 'Zohran Mamdani', 'Bernie Sanders', 'Pete Buttigieg'], 'Republican': ['Greg Abbott', 'Ted Cruz', 'Marco Rubio', 'George Bush']}
```

Appending to the original list is reflected through the dict (mutability):

```python
democrats.append("Barack Obama")
republicans.append("Nikki Haley")
```

To avoid duplicates, check membership first:

```python
name = "Barack Obama"
if name not in political_dictionary["Democrat"]:
    political_dictionary["Democrat"].append(name)
```

You can also append directly through the dictionary:

```python
political_dictionary["Democrat"].append("Ned Lamont")
political_dictionary["Republican"].append("Mike Johnson")
```

```python
print(political_dictionary)
```
```
{'Democrat': ['Kamala Harris', 'Zohran Mamdani', 'Bernie Sanders', 'Pete Buttigieg', 'Barack Obama', 'Ned Lamont'], 'Republican': ['Greg Abbott', 'Ted Cruz', 'Marco Rubio', 'George Bush', 'Nikki Haley', 'Mike Johnson']}
```

```python
print("\nCounts →",
      "Democrat:", len(political_dictionary["Democrat"]),
      "| Republican:", len(political_dictionary["Republican"]))
```
```
Counts → Democrat: 6 | Republican: 6
```

---

## APIs

**What:** An Application Programming Interface is a doorway for apps (and you) to request structured data from a service.

**Why it matters for Text-as-Data:** APIs give you clean JSON you can turn into DataFrames, skip messy scraping, and stay within provider rules.

**How:**

- You send an HTTP request to an endpoint (a URL).
- You include parameters that specify what you want.
- You get a response with a status code and a JSON body (usually).
- You read the JSON, extract fields, and save to CSV.

**Security and keys:**

- Some APIs need an API key. Treat it like a password.
- Do not paste keys into public Colabs or GitHub.
- Keep keys offline or in a private place.

**JSON:** Javascript Object Notation files store structured data in a simple, human-readable way. In Python, use the `json` library to interact with JSON files. JSON → Python maps cleanly: object → dict, array → list, string → str, true/false → True/False, null → None.

To check a website's scraping policy: `root-website-url/robots.txt`. For User-Agent guidance, see: <https://scrapeops.io/web-scraping-playbook/web-scraping-guide-header-user-agents/>

---

## Web Scraping

Load these packages every time you restart Colab:

```python
import requests
from bs4 import BeautifulSoup
import pandas as pd
from time import sleep
```

### The American Presidency Project

The [American Presidency Project](https://www.presidency.ucsb.edu) is a collection of speeches by American presidents and vice-presidents, hosted by the University of California, Santa Barbara. No API needed.

`requests` downloads pages, `BeautifulSoup` parses HTML, and `pandas` turns rows into a table. A `User-Agent` header is included in `headers = {'User-Agent': 'Mozilla/5.0'}` — common practice to indicate browser compatibility; reuse this on every request.

```python
base_url  = 'https://www.presidency.ucsb.edu'
root_page = '/documents/app-categories/spoken-addresses-and-remarks/presidential/eulogies?items_per_page=10'
headers   = {'User-Agent': 'Mozilla/5.0'}

response = requests.get(base_url + root_page, headers=headers)
soup     = BeautifulSoup(response.text, 'html.parser')

# soup.find_all(tagname, attrs={key:value})
```

Extract URLs of all articles on the page:

```python
links = soup.find_all('div', attrs={'class': 'field-title'})
len(links)  # 10
```

```python
links[0]
```
```html
<div class="field-title">
<p><a href="/documents/remarks-memorial-service-for-charlie-kirk-glendale-arizona">Remarks at a Memorial Service for Charlie Kirk in Glendale, Arizona</a></p> </div>
```

```python
# soup's .a.get('href') looks for tag <a> and extracts the href attribute
links[0].a.get('href')
# '/documents/remarks-memorial-service-for-charlie-kirk-glendale-arizona'
```

```python
article_urls = [link.find('a').get('href') for link in links]
```

Before looping everything, fetch one article to make sure selectors work:

```python
article = requests.get(base_url + article_urls[0])
soup    = BeautifulSoup(article.text, "html.parser")
content = soup.find('div', attrs={'class': 'field-docs-content'}).text
```

If links come back empty, the page structure or class name likely changed — inspect and tweak the selector. In a hardened version, wrap these with a small helper that returns `""` when a node is missing to avoid `NoneType` crashes.

Extract all articles and metadata. The `sleep(0.5)` is there to be polite and avoid rate limits — it tells the website you are not a bot, and when scraping many documents it stops the server from crashing:

```python
Speech = []
for url in article_urls:
    article   = requests.get(base_url + url)
    soup_doc  = BeautifulSoup(article.text, "html.parser")
    title     = soup_doc.find('div', attrs={'class': 'field-ds-doc-title'}).text
    date      = soup_doc.find('div', attrs={'class': 'field-docs-start-date-time'}).text
    content   = soup_doc.find('div', attrs={'class': 'field-docs-content'}).text
    candidate = soup_doc.find('h3',  attrs={'class': 'diet-title'}).text
    Speech.append({'title': title, 'candidate': candidate,
                   'content': content, 'date': date, 'url': url})
    sleep(0.5)
```

`Speech` (a list of dicts) becomes a DataFrame:

```python
df_speeches = pd.DataFrame(Speech)
df_speeches
```

Save to Google Drive. Saving to `/content/…csv` also works and shows up in the Colab Files pane:

```python
data_path = '/content/drive/My Drive/'
df_speeches.to_csv(data_path + 'speeches.csv', index=False)
```

### Grabbing All Links Across Pages

For sites with multiple pages, loop over the page parameter. Test one page before looping:

```python
base_url  = 'https://www.presidency.ucsb.edu'
root_page = '/documents/app-categories/spoken-addresses-and-remarks/presidential/eulogies?items_per_page=10&page=0'
headers   = {'User-Agent': 'Mozilla/5.0'}

response = requests.get(base_url + root_page, headers=headers)
soup     = BeautifulSoup(response.text, 'html.parser')
```

```python
links_all = []
for page in range(0, 6):  # Pages 0–5
    page_url = f"/documents/app-categories/spoken-addresses-and-remarks/presidential/eulogies?items_per_page=10&page={page}"
    response = requests.get(base_url + page_url)
    soup     = BeautifulSoup(response.text, 'html.parser')
    links    = soup.find_all('div', attrs={'class': 'field-title'})
    links_all.extend([link.find('a').get('href') for link in links])
    sleep(0.5)

# Note: this method grabs links only, not the speeches themselves.
```
